text: Hi, this is Abu Bakr Ali. I'm presenting a case of a truncal vagotomy in Heineken-Mickles pyloriplasty with a misdiagnosis and a complication.

This is a patient with a complex surgical history. He was involved in a car accident. He had a Missed a bowel injury that led to him undergoing an X-lap. Abdomen was open for two weeks. It was closed again. He leaked again. Abdomen again open for two weeks.
From the car accident, he ended up paraplegic, requiring fixation of his vertebrae. And one of the screws kind of got misplaced and had to be revised.
He also had some sternal fixation, had an aortic transection requiring an aortic graft.

So, um, uh, about seven years later, he presented to the hospital with the, uh, uh, uh, with the perforated ulcer, uh, because the fear of him having all of these surgery, uh, he was treated kind of conservatively. He ended up with, uh, gastric outlet obstruction requiring a PEG tube place and I got involved for an esophageal bad esophageal stricture initial was thought to be cancer turned out to be a peptic stricture and requiring multiple dilation

So I kind of felt the only way of getting him through is fixing his hiatal hernia and dealing with the gastric outlet obstruction. By performing Heineken-Mickle's pyloriplasty felt that the vagotomy will reduce the acid, fixing the hiatal hernia will also reduce the acid reflux.

As mentioned, he's a Jehovah Witness. It took about two hours just to kind of get to this, like to be able to kind of see the hiatus of laparoscopic lysovedesian. So I started with, I dilated him probably about, Uh, six weeks prior to the surgery, uh, there was a mucosal tear, uh, indicating really good dilation, um, and just delayed him for six weeks and very aggressive PPI treatment.

The surgeon performs dissection of the hiatal hernia, initially encountering difficulty locating the posterior vagus nerve. A pin rose is used for retraction and tension, aiding in the identification of the posterior vagus. Both anterior and posterior vagus nerves are visually confirmed and sent for frozen section analysis.

The surgeon identifies the posterior vagus nerve, located to the right of the esophagus in the lower chest. The nerve appears large amidst a significant amount of inflammatory tissue, despite the patient's aggressive PPI management.

Posteriorly was in the problem. I thought there was going to be a lot of adhesions posteriorly due to the fact that he has an aortic stent there.

The surgeon navigates behind the left atrium, encountering a vessel with clear fluid emerging from it. Further exploration is conducted to determine the nature of the vessel, possibly a lymphatic duct, followed by a leak test using an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD).
The surgeon switches the robotic camera to near-infrared mode to visualize the stomach and esophagus. The esophagus appears very inflamed, but there is no visible evidence of a leak.

The surgeon closes the hiatus using Ethabon sutures with Pledgets, ensuring adequate intraabdominal esophageal length after the anterior vagotomy. The duodenum is retracted to the patient's right, revealing the colon beneath. The surgeon performs a Kocher maneuver, exposing the left IVC joining the inferior vena cava. The procedure continues with a Heineken-Michels pyloroplasty, using ICG mode to visualize the common bile duct. Cold scissors are used to separate adhered tissues due to previous perforation.

The surgeon identifies the stricture externally and begins opening the pylorus from the stomach side towards the duodenum. A pinhole obstruction is encountered, prompting the surgeon to open the duodenum to connect both sides. A mass is excised from the stricture site and sent to pathology, revealing pancreatic tissue. The mucosa is re-approximated using vial sutures. The surgeon connects the stomach and duodenum portions, ensuring the common bile duct is not injured. An EGD scope is passed to verify the integrity of the surgical site.

The surgeon performs a Heineken-Michels pyloroplasty, manipulating the pylorus to improve gastric emptying. The procedure is conducted with minimal blood loss, despite the lengthy duration of the surgery.

He was doing very well post-operatively until post-op day number two, where he started getting like short of breath. We got an x-ray. There was a lot of fluid. I immediately placed a chest tube and there was like four liters of fluid coming out from the left side and about a liter coming out of the right side. So I was thinking immediately, did I injure the thoracic duct? So I sent for a triglyceride. It's a send-out test. It takes about five days. I sent the fluid for amylase. The initial fluid amylase was normal. I got a swallow study. The initial swallow study was normal.

and so at this point I proceeded with IR embolization of the thoracic duct they did the study and there was no evidence of leak repeat amylase actually was extremely elevated so it turned out that he had an esophageal injury right where I initially showed you where I was dissecting and it was about a one point five centimeter injury and this was not discovered like it was discovered pretty late I mean He was not on any pressers or anything. I mean, this guy was talking. He was just having like about three liters coming out of his chest tube. It was not bilious. So I proceeded with stenting him. He has a drain. He's on a regular diet, and he's doing pretty much well, and I'll be exchanging the stent very soon. Thank you.
